coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive detection of microRNA-21 by nitrogen-doped carbon dots-based ratio fluorescent probe via nuclease-assisted rolling circle amplification strategy
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Shan
Li, Bo
Mu, Pingping
Zhang, Wenqian
Liu, Yi
Xiao, Qi
coredata.dc:description: Highly sensitive and selective detection of microRNA-21 (miRNA-21) in biological samples is critical for the disease diagnosis and cancer treatment. In this study, a nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs)-based ratio fluorescence sensing strategy was constructed for miRNA-21 detection with high sensitivity and excellent specificity. Bright-blue N-CDs (λ ex/λ em = 378 nm/460 nm) were synthesized by facile one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis method by using uric acid as the single precursor, and the absolute fluorescence quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs were 35.8% and 5.54 ns separately. The padlock probe hybridized with miRNA-21 firstly and then was cyclized by T4 RNA ligase 2 to form a circular template. At the present of dNTPs and phi29 DNA polymerase, the oligonucleotide sequence in miRNA-21 was prolonged to hybridize with the surplus oligonucleotide sequences in circular template, generating long and reduplicated oligonucleotide sequences containing abundant guanine nucleotides. Separate G-quadruplex sequences were generated after the addition of Nt.BbvCI nicking endonuclease, and then hemin bound with G-quadruplex sequence to construct the G-quadruplex DNAzyme. Such G-quadruplex DNAzyme catalyzed the redox reaction of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) with H2O2, finally producing the yellowish-brown 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) (λ em = 562 nm). Due to the inner filter effect between N-CDs and DAP, the ratio fluorescence signal of DAP with N-CDs was utilized for sensitive detection of miRNA-21 with detection limit of 0.87 pM. Such approach has practical feasibility and excellent specificity for miRNA-21 analysis during highly homological miRNA family in HeLa cell lysates and human serum samples.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly sensitive and selective detection of microRNA-21 (miRNA-21) in biological samples is critical for the disease diagnosis and cancer treatment. In this study, a nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs)-based ratio fluorescence sensing strategy was constructed for miRNA-21 detection with high sensitivity and excellent specificity. Bright-blue N-CDs (λ ex/λ em = 378 nm/460 nm) were synthesized by facile one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis method by using uric acid as the single precursor, and the absolute fluorescence quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs were 35.8% and 5.54 ns separately. The padlock probe hybridized with miRNA-21 firstly and then was cyclized by T4 RNA ligase 2 to form a circular template. At the present of dNTPs and phi29 DNA polymerase, the oligonucleotide sequence in miRNA-21 was prolonged to hybridize with the surplus oligonucleotide sequences in circular template, generating long and reduplicated oligonucleotide sequences containing abundant guanine nucleotides. Separate G-quadruplex sequences were generated after the addition of Nt.BbvCI nicking endonuclease, and then hemin bound with G-quadruplex sequence to construct the G-quadruplex DNAzyme. Such G-quadruplex DNAzyme catalyzed the redox reaction of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) with H2O2, finally producing the yellowish-brown 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) (λ em = 562 nm). Due to the inner filter effect between N-CDs and DAP, the ratio fluorescence signal of DAP with N-CDs was utilized for sensitive detection of miRNA-21 with detection limit of 0.87 pM. Such approach has practical feasibility and excellent specificity for miRNA-21 analysis during highly homological miRNA family in HeLa cell lysates and human serum samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a new zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial, possess excellent optical performance, superior aqueous stability, relatively low cytotoxicity, and outstanding biocompatibility [1,2]. Due to their exceptional advantages, CDs find extensive attentions and applications in various research fields, such as photocatalytic degradation, biosensing, cell imaging, and photodynamic therapy [3–6]. Nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) have attracted huge attention owing to their fascinating optical properties and widespread biological applications [5,7]. Until now, several approaches are reported to prepare CDs, including laser ablation method [8], discharge method [9], hydrothermal synthesis [10], solvothermal method [11], and microwave-assisted pyrolysis approach [12,13]. Some of these approaches require relatively harsh synthesis conditions, such as at least two precursors, high synthesis temperature under high pressure, and long reaction time of several hours. Since the microwave-assisted pyrolysis approach can provide homogeneous, simultaneous, and rapid energy to promote the reaction rate under relatively low temperature and pressure, this technique is widely used for the efficient fabrication of N-CDs with homogeneous size and high fluorescence quantum yield (QY). Zhang et al. reported the microwave-assisted pyrolysis approach to prepare N-CDs by using citric acid, tris(hydroxylmethyl)aminomethane, and ethanolamine as carbon and nitrogen sources [14]. Our group also used the microwave-assisted pyrolysis approach to prepare N-CDs by using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors [15]. However, this approach suffers from the relatively complicated process, such as the several heating periods and the usage of two or three kinds of precursors. Therefore, it is still greatly needed to explore facile and green synthesis strategy for N-CDs with superior optical performance via microwave-assisted pyrolysis approach.

As a kind of non-coding and single-stranded RNAs, microRNA (miRNA) plays an important role in several biological processes, and microRNA-21 (miRNA-21) is widely considered as an important clinical biomarker in biological research and diseases diagnosis [16,17]. Since the expression level of miRNA-21 in cancer patients is much higher than that in normal healthy people [18], the sensitive and selective detection of miRNA-21 in biological samples is much important for cancer diagnosis, treatment, and prognosis. Many traditional biological methods have been developed for the detection of miRNA, such as northern blotting technology [19], real-time polymerase chain reaction amplification [20], and miRNA array technology [21]. However, these methods are usually time-consuming, high cost, and low sensitivity in biological applications. To improve the sensitivity of miRNA-21 detection, a variety of methods are proposed, such as surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [22], surface mass spectrometry [23], surface plasmon resonance [24], electrochemical techniques [25–27], chemiluminescence [28,29], and colorimetry [30,31]. However, some above methods need sophisticated process and expensive instruments. Owing to the advantages of relatively cheap instruments, fast response, high sensitivity, and facile operation, fluorescence spectrometry stands out among these detection methods [32–35]. Due to the trace amount of miRNA-21 and the sequence homology of miRNA homologs in biological samples, there is an urgent need for the development of miRNA-21 sensing method with high sensitivity and good specificity. Combining with various signal amplification strategy, several fluorescent sensing methods are reported for highly sensitive and selective detection of miRNA homologs [36–39]. Pu et al. proposed a fluorescence sensing strategy for miRNA let-7a detection based on twin-stage signal amplification of chain displacement reaction and hybrid chain reaction, and the detection limit of miRNA let-7a was as low as 9.01 pM [40]. Xiao et al. constructed a fluorescence platform that combines dual-signal amplification strategies of rolling circle amplification (RCA) and enzyme repair amplification to detect miRNA-21 with detection limit down to 0.2 pM [41]. Although these methods can detect trace miRNA homologs in samples successfully, such single fluorescence change assay may be subject to some external interferences, such as the light scattering of matrix, the fluctuation of detector, and the microenvironment of sample. In contrast, the ratio fluorescence strategy can obviate the disturbances efficiently through measuring the variations of two individual fluorescent signals, leading to its good application for sensitive and specific detection of miRNA-21 [42,43]. These literatures proved the huge application potential of such ratio fluorescence assay for the highly sensitive detection of miRNA-21 in sophisticated matrix.

As far as we known, it is seldom reported about the development of ratio fluorescent approach based on N-CDs for the sensitive and selective detection of miRNA-21 via RCA strategy. Inspired by this, we prepared bright-blue N-CDs (λ ex/λ em = 378 nm/460 nm) through facile one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis method by using nontoxic and biocompatible uric acid (UA) as the single precursor. Such N-CDs and yellowish-brown 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) (λ em = 562 nm) were used to establish the ratio fluorescent probe via inner filter effect. Since hemin could bind with the G-quadruplex sequence to form the G-quadruplex DNAzyme that possessed the mimetic enzymatic activity of horseradish peroxidase [44], DAP was produced after the redox reaction between o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and H2O2 under the catalysis of G-quadruplex DNAzyme. Abundant and separate G-quadruplex sequences were generated via RCA strategy at the present of target miRNA-21 and padlock probe under the enzymatic catalysis of T4 RNA ligase 2, phi29 DNA polymerase, and Nt.BbvCI nicking endonuclease [45,46]. Consequently, the ratio fluorescence signal of DAP with N-CDs was linear with the concentration of miRNA-21, realizing the sensitive detection of miRNA-21. The combination of the specific complementary DNA hybridization and the nuclease-assisted RCA strategy provides an effective practical alternative for the high recognition and the specific detection of miRNA-21 in the homological miRNA family. The feasibility of such ratio fluorescence assay was also demonstrated by detecting miRNA-21 in HeLa cell lysates and human serum samples.

UA, OPD, DAP, diethyl pyrocarbonate (DEPC), 30% hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and hemin were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Reagents Company (https://www.sigmaaldrich.com/). Other chemicals were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (https://www.reagent.com.cn). T4 RNA ligase 2 and Nt.BbvCI nicking endonuclease were purchased from New England Biolabs Inc. (https://www.neb.cn/). Phi29 DNA polymerase, dNTPs, and all oligonucleotides were purchased from Sangon Biological Engineering Technological Co., Ltd (https://www.sangon.com/). The sequences of these oligonucleotides were as follows:

Padlock probe: 5′-PO4- CTG ATA AGC TA T TAC C↓TC AGC CCT AAC CCT AAC CCT AAC CCT TAC CCT AAC CCT AAC CCT AAC CC↓T CAG C
TC AAC ATC AGT -OH-3’;
MiRNA-21: 5′-UAG CUU AUC AGA CUG AUG UUGA-3’;
MiRNA-141: 5′-UAA CAC UGU CUG GUA AAG AUGG-3’;
MiRNA let-7a: 5′-UGA GGU AGU AGG UUG UAU AGUU-3’;
Single-base mismatched miRNA (S-RNA): 5′-UAG CUU AUC GGA CUG AUG UUGA-3’;
Triple-base mismatched miRNA (T-RNA): 5′-UUG CUU AUC GGA CUG AUC UUGA-3’.

In brief, 0.05 g UA was dissolved in 3 mL ultrapure water and then the mixture was transferred into the CEM Discover Benchmate microwave reactor with heating temperature of 170 °C for 25 min. After reaction, the solution was cooled down to room temperature. The sediment was removed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was filtered by using 0.22 μm microporous filter membrane. The liquid product was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (cut-off molecular weight of 1000 Da) for 24 h, and then the solution in the dialysis bag was freeze-dried to obtain the solid N-CDs.

Transmission emission microscope (TEM) image and energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) were obtained by JEM 2011 field emission transmission electron microscope (https://www.jeol.co.jp/). Bruker D & Advance X-ray diffractometer (https://www.bruker.com/) was used to record X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) curve. Thermo Escalab 250 Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (https://www.thermo.com.cn/) was used to test X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). UV–vis absorption spectrum was measured on Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer (http://www.shimadzu.com/). Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR) was measured on Thermo Fourier infrared spectrometer (https://www.thermo.com.cn/). Fluorescence spectra were obtained by Thermal Lumina fluorescence spectrophotometer (https://www.thermo.com.cn/). Time-resolved fluorescence spectra were obtained on Horiba QM 8075 spectrometer (https://www.horiba.com/). Circular dichroism (CD) spectra were recorded on Applied Photophysics Chirascan-type CD spectrometry (https://www.photophysics.com/). The pH value of solution was adjusted on Sartorius PB-10 pH meter (https://www.sartorius.com/).

The absolute fluorescence QY and fluorescence lifetime of N-CD were obtained on Horiba QM 8075 spectrometer. The absolute fluorescence QY of N-CDs was calculated according to the equation of QY = ∫L Emission/(∫E Solvent - ∫E Sample). The L Emission is the number of emitted photons of N-CDs. The E Solvent and E Sample represent the number of excited photons of solvent and N-CD, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs was calculated by double exponential fitting formula of <τ > = (∑τ i 2 b i)/(∑τ i b i). The τ is the fluorescence decay time of N-CDs and b is the normalized pre-exponential factor.

Firstly, 10 μL of miRNA-21 with different concentration was mixed with 10 μL of padlock probe (50 nM) at 60 °C for 10 min. Then, 10 μL of 10 × T4 RNA ligase 2 reaction buffer and 10 μL of T4 RNA ligase 2 (2 U/μL) were added subsequently, and the mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. After that, 10 μL of 10 × phi29 DNA polymerase reaction buffer, 10 μL of phi29 DNA polymerase (1.0 U/μL), 10 μL of dNTP (500 μM), 10 μL of Nt.BbvCI nicking endonuclease (0.5 U/μL), and 20 μL of DEPC-treated ultrapure water were added subsequently. The mixture was further incubated at 37 °C for 2 h to proceed the RCA reaction. After the RCA reaction, 10 μL of hemin (1 mM) was added into the mixture for additional 15 min at room temperature to construct the G-quadruplex DNAzyme. Then, 10 μL of OPD (5 mM), 10 μL of H2O2 (2 mM), 100 μL of N-CDs (420 μg/mL), and 470 μL of phosphate buffer saline (0.1 M, pH 7.4) were added subsequently to react at room temperature for 15 min. For the preparation of phosphate buffer saline (0.1 M, pH 7.4), 1.7913 g of Na2HPO4·12H2O, 0.1368 g of KH2PO4, 0.1009 g of KCl, and 4.0012 g of NaCl were dissolved in 500 mL of ultrapure water at room temperature. Finally, the fluorescence spectrum was recorded under the excitation wavelength of 378 nm, and the fluorescence intensities at 460 nm and 562 nm were recorded to detect miRNA-21.

The 10% native polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis was taken place in 0.5 × Tris-H3BO3 buffer (44.5 mM) with constant voltage of 100 V for 1 h. The gel was scanned on the Miulab gel-630 imaging analysis system (http://www.miulab.com/).

Human blood samples were taken from healthy adult male and female volunteers in our laboratory. Firstly, the fresh blood samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 8 min. Then, 1 mL of human serum sample was mixed with 15 mL of perchloric acid, the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 5 min to precipitate big proteins. After that, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was collected and stored at 4 °C before detection. The pre-treated human serum samples were diluted for 50 times with phosphate buffer saline (0.1 M, pH 7.4) before detection.

Human cervical cancer HeLa cells were purchased from Shanghai Gaining Biological Co., Ltd (www.shgnsw.com). HeLa cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium at 37 °C for 24 h in the environment with humidity of 5% CO2 and 95% air. Firstly, 40 μL of trichloromethane and 200 μL of DEPC-treated ultrapure water were added into the cell culture solution, and the mixture was shaken vigorously at room temperature for 30 s. Then, the solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min to collect the supernatant. After that, 100 mL of isopropanol was added into the supernatant, and the mixture was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min to precipitate RNA. Total RNA was dissolved in phosphate buffer saline (0.1 M, pH 7.4) before detection.

Preliminary experimental results indicated that our N-CDs possess a high absolute fluorescence QY of 35.8% via one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis method under the optimal preparation conditions (Fig. S1). The fluorescence QY of such N-CDs is much higher than those of some reported N-CDs prepared through hydrothermal synthesis method (less than 30%) [5,7,47], validating the apparent advantage of microwave-assisted pyrolysis approach. The optical properties of N-CDs were explored by UV–vis absorption spectrometry and fluorescence spectrometry. As shown in Fig. 1 A, N-CDs exhibit two absorption peaks at 360 nm and 397 nm, respectively, which are caused by the n → π* electron transition of CO bond and C-N bond, respectively [48]. The strong bright-blue fluorescence of N-CDs can be observed clearly after irradiation under 365 nm UV lamp (inset of Fig. 1A). As exhibited in Fig. 1B, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs is increased gradually when the excitation wavelength is prolonged from 350 nm to 378 nm but is decreased dramatically if the excitation wavelength is increased from 378 nm to 400 nm. Since the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs reaches to its maximum value when the excitation wavelength is 378 nm, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of N-CDs are 378 nm and 460 nm, respectively. When the excitation wavelength is changed from 350 nm to 400 nm, the emission peak of N-CDs shows a slight red-shift (∼4 nm) that can be omitted, suggesting the excitation-independent emission property of N-CDs [10,15]. Furthermore, N-CDs have an average fluorescence lifetime of 5.54 ns (Fig. S2) and distinguish optical stability under some extreme test conditions (Fig. S3), implying the outstanding fluorescence performance of N-CDs in future biological application fields.

As shown in TEM image, N-CDs are approximately spherical in shape, and N-CDs have excellent dispersion in aqueous solution without obvious aggregation (Fig. 1C). The EDS spectrum of N-CDs confirms the existences of C, N, and O elements, and the weight percentages of these elements are 89.7% of C, 5.7% of N, and 4.6% of O (Fig. S4). The crystal lattice spacing of one particle is about 0.21 nm (Inset in Fig. 1C), which is consistent with the (100) crystal plane of graphite carbon [5,7]. Through counting about one hundred particles, the size distribution of N-CDs is ranged from 2.2 nm to 5.0 nm, and the average particle size of N-CDs is about 3.56 nm (Fig. 1D), implying the narrow size distribution of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1E, N-CDs show a characteristic diffraction peak at 2θ = 23.1°, which is attributed to the (002) crystal plane of the graphite carbon [5,7]. Such results validate the main graphite structure of these N-CDs [5,7]. As shown in the FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 1F), the sharp and strong absorption peaks at 3432.94 cm−1 and 3178.48 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H bond and N-H bond, respectively [47]. The sharp but small absorption peaks at 2922.12 cm−1 and 2851.85 cm−1 are generated by the stretching vibration of C-H bond [48]. The absorption peaks at 1664.40 cm−1, 1385.99 cm−1, and 1120.43 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of CO bond, the bending vibration of C-N bond and C-O bond, respectively [49,50]. The FT-IR result proves the successful doping of N atoms in N-CDs.

The surface composition of N-CDs was continuously researched by XPS. As clearly seen in Fig. 2 A, N-CDs are mainly composed of C, N, and O elements, and three characteristic binding energy peaks are located at 284.81 eV of C 1s, 400.36 eV of N 1s, and 532.28 eV of O 1s, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s shows three binding energy peaks at 284.80 eV, 286.18 eV, and 288.90 eV (Fig. 2B), corresponding to the C-C/CC bond, C-N bond, and CO bond, respectively [51]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1s presents three binding energy peaks of 399.74 eV, 400.42 eV, and 401.56 eV (Fig. 2C), corresponding to the N-H bond, N-(C3) bond, and C-N-C bond, respectively [52,53]. As shown in Fig. 2D, the high-resolution XPS of O 1s has three binding energy peaks at 531.15 eV, 532.08 eV, and 532.93 eV, belonging to the CO bond, C-O bond, and OC-O bond, respectively [54]. The FT-IR and XPS results confirm that plentiful oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups, such as hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl, are existed in N-CDs, finally leading to their remarkable aqueous solubility for further biological applications.

As shown in Scheme 1 , the padlock probe hybridizes with miRNA-21 and then is cyclized by T4 RNA ligase 2 to form a circular template for subsequent RCA reaction. After the addition of dNTPs and phi29 DNA polymerase, the oligonucleotide sequence in miRNA-21 is prolonged to hybridize with the surplus oligonucleotide sequences in the circular template [45], generating long and reduplicated oligonucleotide sequences containing abundant guanine nucleotides via RCA reaction. The padlock probe contains the recognition nicking site of nicking endonuclease, so separate G-quadruplex sequences are generated after the addition of Nt.BbvCI nicking endonuclease [55]. Since the CD spectrum of G-quadruplex sequence shows a strong positive absorption peak around 290 nm and a tiny negative absorption peak near 240 nm after the addition of hemin (Fig. S5), such G-quadruplex sequence forms the distinct hybrid structure in the presence of hemin [56,57]. Consequently, hemin binds with the G-quadruplex sequence to construct the G-quadruplex DNAzyme, which can catalyze the redox reaction between H2O2 and OPD to produce fluorescent DAP [58,59]. Finally, the fluorescent DAP can react with N-CDs to affect their fluorescence, leading to the ratio fluorescent probe for the highly sensitive and selective detection of miRNA-21.

The interaction between N-CDs and DAP is the vital factor that affects the performance of this ratio fluorescent probe. As exhibited in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence decay curve of N-CDs is seldom affected by DAP when its concentration is increased from 1 μM to 50 μM. The average fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs keeps almost unchanged when the concentration of DAP is in the range of 1 μM–50 μM, which can exclude the electron transfer, the energy transfer, and the dynamic quenching processes during the N-CDs/DAP system [58,59]. As further shown in Fig. 3B, DAP exhibits a characteristic absorption peak near 430 nm, and the absorption spectrum of N-CDs/DAP is increased obviously compared with those of N-CDs alone and DAP alone. However, the differential spectrum of N-CDs/DAP system with N-CDs is almost the same with the absorption spectrum of DAP, further excluding the static quenching mode in the N-CDs/DAP system [58,59]. In addition, the Zeta potential values of N-CDs, DAP, and N-CDs/DAP are measured to be +2.3 mV, +13.4 mV, and +14.4 mV, respectively (Fig. S6). So, the obvious electrostatic repulsion is existed in the N-CDs/DAP system due to both the positively charged N-CDs and DAP, which also exclude the static quenching mechanism in the N-CDs/DAP system [60]. As shown in Fig. 3C, OPD does not show any optical absorption ability in the wavelength range of 330 nm–620 nm, while the UV–vis absorption spectrum of DAP overlaps with both the emission spectrum and the excitation spectrum of N-CDs to some extent. Therefore, DAP can not only absorb partial excitation light of 378 nm from the instrument but also absorb most emission light of N-CDs at 460 nm. Comparably, the optical absorption ability of DAP at 460 nm is much stronger than that at 378 nm, thus DAP mainly absorbs the emission light of N-CDs to affect their fluorescence intensity. All these results confirm the mainly inner filter effect during the interaction process of N-CDs with DAP, which is highly agreed with the reported interaction mechanism among DAP and carbon-based nanoparticles [58,59].

RCA process is vital for the sensitive and selective detection of miRNA-21 by using this ratio fluorescent probe. Fig. 3D exhibits the polyacrylamide gel image of different oligonucleotides products. Since the base numbers of padlock probe and miRNA-21 are 81 and 22 separately, the bright migration band with base number of around 100 is mainly ascribed to the padlock probe (lane 1 in Fig. 3D), while the inconspicuous migration band with smaller base number (from 25 to 50) may be attributed to the target miRNA-21 (lane 1 in Fig. 3D). Since both miRNA-21 and padlock probe are existed in solution separately, the efficient DNA hybridization is not taken place between them. After adding T4 RNA ligase 2, the padlock probe hybridizes with miRNA-21 to form a circular template efficiently, thus the amount of miDNA-21 is decreased obviously (lane 2 in Fig. 3D). After adding dNTPs and phi29 DNA polymerase, the oligonucleotide sequence of miRNA-21 is prolonged to hybridize with the surplus oligonucleotide sequences in circular template [45], generating long and reduplicated oligonucleotide sequences containing abundant guanine nucleotides via RCA reaction. Since long and reduplicated oligonucleotide sequences are produced and the base numbers of oligonucleotides are increased dramatically, two bright migration bands are appeared at the sample hole and at the position with base number of more than 100, respectively (lane 3 in Fig. 3D). When Nt.BbvCI nicking endonuclease is added, separate G-quadruplex sequences are produced from the long and reduplicated oligonucleotide sequences, resulting in several diffused migration bands with base number of more than 100 (lane 4 in Fig. 3D). This phenomenon validates the successful RCA reaction illustrated in Scheme 1.

In order to verify the selectivity and the feasibility of our ratio fluorescence strategy for miRNA-21 analysis, the interferences of these components on the fluorescence spectrum of N-CDs were investigated systematically. As illustrated in the curve 1 in Fig. 4 A, N-CDs emit strong fluorescence at 460 nm under the excitation of 378 nm. The fluorescence spectrum of N-CDs remains almost unchanged after the addition of miRNA-21, padlock probe, T4 RNA ligase 2, phi29 DNA polymerase, dNTPs, Nt.BbvCI nicking endonuclease, OPD, and H2O2 (curves 2 to 9 in Fig. 4A). According to the curve 10 in Fig. 4A, hemin can reduce the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs by about 12.5%. Moreover, when only one specific component (except Nt.BbvCI nicking endonuclease) is absent but hemin is present in the solution, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs is reduced by around 13% (curves 11 to 15, 17, and 18 in Fig. 4A). When hemin, OPD, and H2O2 are mixed with N-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs is reduced by around 13.1% (curve 27 in Fig. 4A). On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs is unchanged when hemin is absent in the solution (curves 19 to 25 in Fig. 4A). All these results imply that only hemin can affect the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. The preliminary experimental result suggested that hemin possesses optical absorption ability in the wavelength range of 300 nm–430 nm (Fig. S7), so hemin may absorb the excitation light to influence the fluorescence of N-CDs. However, due to the absent or the less amount of DAP, the fluorescence intensity at 562 nm belonging to DAP is much tiny (curves 1 to 15, 17 to 25, and 27 in Fig. 4A). The corresponding fluorescence intensities at 460 nm (purple columns) and 562 nm (blue columns) of the solution containing different components are existed in Fig. 4B. According to the columns (except columns 16 and 26) in Fig. 4C, the ratio fluorescence signal I 562/I 460 values are all around 0.10. It is obvious that the ratio fluorescence signal I 562/I 460 is not affected by these co-existing components no matter hemin is present or absent, which further demonstrates the excellent anti-jamming capability of this ratio fluorescence strategy from the co-existing components, leading to its sensitive and specific detection of miRNA-21.

As shown in the curve 16 in Fig. 4A and the column 16 in Fig. 4B, when these components are all existed in solution except Nt.BbvCI nicking endonuclease, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs is quenched by 30.9% and the fluorescence signal of DAP is appeared. When 700 pM of miRNA-21 is present, the ratio fluorescence signal I 562/I 460 is much low to be about 0.33 (column 16 in Fig. 4C). Since long and reduplicated oligonucleotide sequences containing abundant guanine nucleotides are generated via RCA reaction, the formation of G-quadruplex DNAzyme may be inhibited partially due to the obvious steric-hinerance effect of such long oligonucleotide sequences, finally restraining the production of DAP. Fortunately, Nt.BbvCI nicking endonuclease can promote the generation of separate G-quadruplex sequences and then facilitate the construction of G-quadruplex DNAzyme, resulting in the remarkable variations on the fluorescence intensities of both N-CDs and DAP. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 460 nm is decreased to be about 27.3% of its initial intensity, and the fluorescence intensity of DAP is doubled inversely (curve 26 in Fig. 4A and column 26 in Fig. 4B). Comparably, the ratio fluorescence signal I 562/I 460 is increased dramatically to 1.63 with the addition of Nt.BbvCI nicking endonuclease (column 26 in Fig. 4C), which not only validates the enzymatic activity of Nt.BbvCI nicking endonuclease but also confirms the significant amplification efficiency of our ratio fluorescence strategy. All these results suggest that our approach possesses high sensitivity and excellent selectivity in the detection of miRNA-21.

Under the optimal experimental conditions (Fig. S8), the sensitivity of this approach for miRNA-21 detection was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 5 A, with the addition of miRNA-21 from 5 pM to 2 nM, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 460 nm is decreased gradually, and the fluorescence intensity of DAP at 562 nm is increased step by step conversely. When the concentration of miRNA-21 is in the range of 5 pM–500 pM, the ratio fluorescence signal I 562/I 460 is linearly correlated with the concentration of miRNA-21, and the linear regression equation is I 562/I 460 = 1.64 × 10−3 [miRNA-21] (pM) + 0.158 with correlation coefficient R 2 = 0.997 (Fig. 5B). Through the calculation formula of LOD = 3σ/S, the detection limit of miRNA-21 is as low as 0.87 pM. As shown in Fig. 5C, with the increment of the concentration of miRNA-21 from 0 to 500 pM, the color of the solution is gradually changed from colorless to yellow, and the emitted light color is changed from bright blue to mustard under 365 nm UV light irradiation. Thus, our ratio fluorescent probe can realize the highly sensitive analysis of miRNA-21 both by naked eyes and by fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Table 1 , our approach possesses an acceptable linear range and relatively lower detection limit compared with some reported methods for miRNA detection to our knowledge. The satisfactory sensitivity of our approach may be ascribed to the following two reasons: (1) the high efficiency of this nuclease-assisted RCA strategy for the generation of abundant and reduplicated G-quadruplex sequences; (2) the high efficiency of such G-quadruplex DNAzyme for the catalysis of the redox reaction between OPD and H2O2.

Detection specificity is another crucial factor to evaluate the sensing assay since the miRNA family often contains closely related sequences with high homology. The specificity of our strategy was investigated by detecting several homologous sequences of the miRNA family, including target miRNA-21, miRNA-141, miRNA let-7a, single-base mismatched miRNA (S-RNA), and triple-base mismatched miRNA (T-RNA). As shown in Fig. 6 A, the ratio fluorescence signal I 562/I 460 without any miRNAs is about 0.28, but the ratio fluorescence signal I 562/I 460 changes to some extent after the addition of different miRNA homologs. The ratio fluorescence signal I 562/I 460 caused by S-RNA, T-RNA, miRNA-141, miRNA let-7a, and miRNA-21 are about 0.35, 0.32, 0.29, 0.29, and 1.00, respectively. Comparably, the ratio fluorescence signal I 562/I 460 caused by miRNA-21 is about three times higher than those caused by other miRNA homologs. These results demonstrate that our ratio fluorescence approach has high specificity for miRNA-21 detection in complicated samples and can distinguish a single base variation toward target miRNA-21 among homologous miRNA family.

The concentration of miRNA-21 in human serum samples was firstly detected to verify the potential applicability of our probe in actual samples analysis. The pre-treated human serum samples were diluted for 50 times with phosphate buffer saline (0.1 M, pH 7.4). It is obvious that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs keeps almost unchanged even after incubation in these human serum samples for 24 h (Fig. 6B), implying the potential application of such N-CDs in actual human samples. The miRNA-21 stock solution was added into the diluted human serum samples to make its final concentration be 10, 50, and 100 pM, respectively. The recovery of miRNA-21 in these human serum samples was tested through the standard addition recovery method via the linear regression equation. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery of miRNA-21 is in the range of 96.6%–105.1%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is between 1.6% and 4.0%. The results confirm that such detection approach has excellent applicability for miRNA-21 analysis in complicated human serum samples.

To verify the potential feasibility of our probe for miRNA-21 detection in actual samples, this method was further used to detect the amount of miRNA-21 extracted from HeLa cells. As shown in Fig. 6C, the absorbance of the total RNA extracted from HeLa cells at 260 nm is 2.02 times higher than that at 280 nm, while the absorbance of the total RNA extracted from HeLa cells at 260 nm is 1.89 times higher than that at 230 nm. Such results suggest that the total RNA extracted from HeLa cells is free of protein and DNA pollution [61]. The miRNA-21 stock solution was added into the total RNA samples to make the final concentration of the spiked miRNA-21 be 10, 20, 50, 100, and 200 pM, respectively. Through the standard addition recovery method and the linear regression equation, the concentration of miRNA-21 in the total RNA extracted from HeLa cells is calculated to be 11.9 pM (Fig. 6D), which is in the normal concentration range reported in other literatures [34]. This result indicates that our ratio fluorescent probe can detect miRNA-21 in sophisticated biological system effectively and accurately.

In summary, we used one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis method to synthesize N-CDs with absolute fluorescence QY of 35.8% and fluorescence lifetime of 5.54 ns by using UA as the single precursor. Combining the nuclease-assisted RCA reaction, a ratio fluorescent probe composed of bright-blue N-CDs (λ ex/λ em = 378 nm/460 nm) and yellowish-brown DAP (λ em = 562 nm) was constructed to detect miRNA-21 sensitively with detection limit of as low as 0.87 pM. The specific complementary DNA hybridization provides high selectivity for the identification miRNA-21 in the highly homological miRNA family with only single base mismatch. This work provides a sensitive, selective, and accurate method for detecting miRNA-21 in HeLa cell lysates and human serum samples, which exhibits huge potential application in biological-related areas. To efficiently omit the interference of the auto fluorescence in biological matrix and further expand the biological application field of CDs, we are devoted to the exploration and the exploitation of CDs with longer wavelength emission.

Shan Huang: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Bo Li: Methodology, Investigation. Pingping Mu: Investigation, Validation. Wenqian Zhang: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yi Liu: Investigation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Qi Xiao: Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
